title: ExcelFormer: A Neural Network Surpassing GBDTs on Tabular Data
authors: Jintai Chen, Jiahuan Yan (co-first); Qiyuan Chen, Danny Z. Chen, Jian Wu, Jimeng Sun (UIUC, Zhejiang University, Univ. of Notre Dame)
tokenization_method: FT-Transformer-style per-feature tokenizer with a GLU twist. Pipeline: (1) PRE-PROCESS each column outside the network — numerical features go through sklearn QuantileTransformer; categorical features are converted to SCALARS via the CatBoost target-encoder BEFORE the model sees them. So every column enters the network as a single real number f_i. (2) EMBED each scalar f_i into a d-dim token z_i with a per-feature gated linear unit: z_i = tanh(f_i W_{i,1} + b_{i,1}) ⊙ (f_i W_{i,2} + b_{i,2}), W_{i,*} in R^{1×d}, b_{i,*} in R^d. This replaces FT-Transformer's plain affine z_i = f_i W_i + b_i. (3) Stack the f tokens into z^(0) in R^{f×d} (NO [CLS] token; the head pools over all f tokens). The network's novelty is NOT the tokenizer but what processes the tokens: alternating Semi-Permeable Attention (SPA) blocks and GLU 'attentive FFN' blocks, L=3 by default, d=256, 32 heads.
feature_types_handled: numerical and categorical (mixed), but UNIFIED to scalars before tokenization. No native categorical embedding — categoricals are CatBoost-target-encoded to a single number per cell, then treated exactly like a numerical feature. No native missing-value handling (relies on upstream imputation). No temporal / time-varying support — each row is an unordered set of static columns.
token_granularity: per-feature, per-value: one token per input column, sequence length = f (number of features), no [CLS] token. Each token is a value-aware d-dim embedding of one (already-scalarized) feature value.
numerical_strategy: QuantileTransformer (to a normal/uniform target distribution) applied per column outside the net, then a per-feature GLU embedding z_i = tanh(f_i W_{i,1}+b_{i,1}) ⊙ (f_i W_{i,2}+b_{i,2}). This is a richer-than-affine but still single-scalar-in embedding — NOT piecewise-linear (PLR), NOT periodic/Fourier, NOT binned. The 'richness' is the multiplicative gate over the embedding dimension, not extra input resolution.
categorical_strategy: CatBoost (ordered target/leave-one-out) encoding to a scalar, done in sklearn category_encoders BEFORE the network. There is NO learned categorical embedding table inside ExcelFormer — categoricals and numericals are indistinguishable to the model after preprocessing.
high_cardinality_handling: implicit only, via the CatBoost encoder: any cardinality collapses to one real number per cell, so there is zero parameter or sequence-length growth with cardinality. But this also means no learned per-category representation and full reliance on the target-encoding quality (target leakage risk, cold-start on rare/unseen categories, and the encoding is fixed before training). No hashing, no learned codes.
temporal_handling: none. No patching, recurrence, channel-as-token, or static/temporal fusion. The 'AAPL_stock_price' regression datasets in the benchmark are treated as plain i.i.d. tabular rows, not time series.
scales_to_many_features: WEAK / unsupported above ~50 features. SPA and the GLU-FFN are explicitly designed to add NO compute over FT-Transformer, but the core attention is still a full f×f masked softmax — O(f^2) in feature count, same wall as FT-Transformer. The masking trims information FLOW, not COST. Crucially, the largest dataset in the entire paper has 54 features (covtype, jannis); the 96 'small' datasets are mostly <30 features. The paper makes NO claim and shows NO experiment at 70+ features, so any '70+' applicability is extrapolation, not evidence. SPA's benefit is data-efficiency / noise-robustness, not scaling.
downstream_model: Transformer encoder, ~FT-Transformer size: L=3 blocks of [SPA multi-head attention (32 heads) + GLU attentive FFN], d=256, attention-map dropout 0.3. Prediction head = two linear layers (one over the feature axis Linear_f, one over the embedding axis Linear_d) with a P-ReLU between, pooling all f tokens. AdamW, lr 1e-4, no weight decay, early-stop patience 32.
pretraining_objective: none. Fully supervised end-to-end (cross-entropy for classification, MSE for regression). No self-supervision or transfer. The paper explicitly argues cross-table pretraining (XTab, TransTab) gives little benefit due to table heterogeneity, and includes them only as weak baselines.
key_results: On 96 small datasets (<10k rows) ExcelFormer ranks best (mean rank ~3.2-3.6 Mix/Hid-Mix tuned) ahead of tuned XGBoost (~4.2), CatBoost (~4.6) and FT-Transformer (~4.3) — AND beats those tuned baselines even with DEFAULT (untuned) ExcelFormer hyperparameters (just tuning the Mixup coefficient). On 21 larger datasets (>10k) it again ranks #1 (~2.0-2.4 tuned vs XGBoost ~4.3, FT-T ~5.2). Stratified by task type / size / feature count it wins every subgroup except small-feature (<8) regression where tuned XGBoost edges it. Headline: a single fixed-HP DNN that beats tuned GBDTs on both GBDT-favored (small) and DNN-favored (large) tables.
baselines_compared: GBDT: XGBoost, CatBoost (both tuned harder than usual — up to 4096 trees, 500 tuning iters). DNN: FT-Transformer, SAINT, MLP, DCN-v2, AutoInt, TabPFN. Pretrained: TransTab, XTab (default HPs, weak). Data-aug ablation vs Mixup, CutMix on both FT-Transformer and ExcelFormer backbones.
transferable_idea: Two cleanly separable, low-cost inductive biases for noisy wide-ish tables: (1) Semi-Permeable Attention — precompute a per-feature importance score (normalized mutual information with the target) and add a STATIC additive mask M to attention logits so that a more-informative feature is forbidden from attending to (reading from) a less-informative one (M[i,j]=-inf iff I(f_i)>I(f_j)), letting signal flow up the importance order but blocking noise from contaminating strong features; paired with 'interaction-attenuated initialization' (scale all SPA weights by gamma=1e-4 so the model starts near a no-interaction, rotationally-VARIANT MLP and grows interactions data-drivenly). (2) Feat-Mix / Hid-Mix — Mixup variants that SWAP whole features (Feat-Mix) or whole embedding dimensions (Hid-Mix) between two rows and set the label-mix weight by summed feature importance, avoiding the over-smoothing of convex (vanilla) Mixup on irregular tabular targets. Both are tokenizer-agnostic and bolt onto any FT-Transformer-style stack.
limitations: Authors-stated: must pick Feat-Mix OR Hid-Mix per dataset (their combination underperforms); the importance mask uses a fixed MI estimate. Implied / found on read: (a) NO native categorical or high-cardinality tokenization — categoricals are CatBoost-encoded to scalars upstream, so the much-touted 'feature tokenizer' is numerical-only inside the net; (b) NO temporal/static fusion, NO missing-value handling; (c) attention is still O(f^2) — SPA does not reduce cost, so no scaling advantage for wide tables; (d) max 54 features in all experiments — zero evidence at 70+; (e) the SPA mask is a strict total order on features computed BEFORE training and never updated.
pitfalls: 1) MARKETING vs MECHANISM: it is sold as a tabular *Transformer* with a *feature tokenizer*, but the tokenizer is essentially FT-Transformer's (plus a GLU gate) and ALL categorical structure is destroyed by CatBoost-encoding to scalars before the net — do NOT cite this as a categorical/high-cardinality tokenization method. 2) SPA solves DATA-EFFICIENCY (rotational variance), NOT SCALING; people conflate 'masked attention' with 'efficient attention' — the f×f softmax cost is unchanged. 3) The static MI-based importance mask is brittle: MI estimates are noisy with few samples and many features, ties/near-ties make the strict > vs <= mask arbitrary, and the order is frozen at t=0 — at 70+ correlated features the ranking (and thus which feature may inform which) is unstable and unvalidated. 4) CatBoost target-encoding before the model invites TARGET LEAKAGE if not done with proper out-of-fold scheme, and cold-starts on unseen/rare categories — a real risk for high-cardinality fields. 5) 'No tuning needed' is overstated: you still tune the Mixup alpha (Mix Tuned), and the comparison gives baselines a fixed protocol from FT-Transformer's paper rather than per-baseline-optimal pipelines; benchmark is single-split 80/20 (no cross-val), so rank gaps within ~0.5 are not clearly significant. 6) The 'AAPL stock price' datasets are time series treated as i.i.d. rows — a benchmark-hygiene smell, not temporal modeling.
relevance_to_topic: Directly in the tabular-transformers route and a sibling of FT-Transformer, but its real contribution for the 70+-heterogeneous-feature setting is the two BOLT-ON regularizers (importance-masked SPA + importance-weighted feature/dimension Mixup), not its tokenizer — which actually REGRESSES on this topic's needs by scalarizing all categoricals and ignoring time. Useful as: an inductive-bias to make a wide-feature transformer robust to many uninformative columns, and a data-augmentation recipe; NOT useful as a categorical/high-cardinality/temporal tokenizer.
code_available: yes — official PyTorch repo https://github.com/whatashot/excelformer (SPA, Feat-Mix/Hid-Mix, run scripts; ~68 stars, ~18 commits, research-grade, no releases, maintenance unclear). Datasets mirrored at https://huggingface.co/datasets/jyansir/excelformer.
